region_of_origin: China (Cantonese origin); widely adopted in Singapore, Philippines, and Southeast Asia

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general wonton entry; no specific 'fried wonton ball' form exists. closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers fried dumplings as a category; no specific wonton ball sub-entry exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for fried wonton ball or equivalent fried dumpling snack.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fried wonton balls consists of approximately 6 pieces weighing around 90 g, commonly served as a snack or appetiser.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Wonton / Deep-fried Wonton — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Wontons, fried (NDB 58146) and analogous deep-fried dumpling data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried wontons (NDB 58146) used as primary reference. Values cross-checked against USDA Foundation Foods entries for fried dumplings and peer-reviewed nutrient composition data for deep-fried wheat-wrapped meat dumplings. Per-100g values are adapted from the SR Legacy fried wonton entry with minor adjustments for the ball-shaped form factor (similar filling and wrapper composition). No exact Singapore-specific entry found in HPB Foodo for this item.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of wheat-based fried dumplings and wonton wrappers — analogous estimate based on fried dim sum / wheat dumpling GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for fried wonton balls found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on analogous fried wheat-wrapped dumplings and dim sum items listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. The wheat wrapper contributes the primary glycemic load; deep-frying moderately slows starch digestion. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 23.85) / 100 ≈ 13.1, where carbohydrate per 90 g serving = 26.5 × 0.9 = 23.85 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (480 mg per 100 g; approximately 432 mg per 90 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit fried wonton ball consumption due to the high sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning, which can contribute significantly to daily sodium limits of 2000 mg; portion control to 2–3 pieces and avoidance of dipping sauces is advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 55) and moderate carbohydrate load from wheat wonton wrappers
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes can consume fried wonton balls in moderation as the low GI (55) and moderate glycemic load (GL ≈ 13 per serving) have a milder impact on postprandial blood glucose compared to high GI foods; portion control and pairing with non-starchy vegetables are still recommended.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated total fat (14.8 g/100 g), saturated fat (3.5 g/100 g), cholesterol (42 mg/100 g), and high sodium from deep-frying and seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit fried wonton ball intake due to the combined burden of saturated fat, dietary cholesterol, and high sodium, all of which are associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes; occasional consumption in small portions is preferable to regular intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried wonton balls (also spelled fried wanton balls) are bite-sized deep-fried snacks made from thin wheat-flour wonton wrappers filled with a seasoned mixture of minced pork and shrimp, shaped into a ball or compact parcel and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The exterior is light and crunchy while the interior remains moist and savoury. They are a popular snack and appetiser in Singapore, the Philippines, and across Southeast Asia, with roots in Cantonese Chinese cuisine. Nutritionally, per 100 g they provide approximately 272 kcal, 10.2 g protein, 14.8 g fat (including 3.5 g saturated fat), and 26.5 g carbohydrate, with a notable sodium content of 480 mg. The low glycemic index (estimated GI 55) and moderate glycemic load make them a food that can be consumed in moderation for individuals managing blood glucose. The high sodium and saturated fat content are relevant concerns for those with hypertension and cardiovascular disease. They are commonly served with chilli sauce or sweet-and-sour dipping sauce and appear as a starter, snack, or side dish in Chinese-Filipino and Singaporean Chinese restaurant menus.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fried wonton balls?
What is the GI of fried wontons?
Can diabetics eat fried wonton balls?
What is inside a fried wonton ball?
Are fried wonton balls high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for fried wonton balls found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fried wonton entry (NDB 58146), which is the closest approved-source match. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for similar fried wheat-wrapped dumplings. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the absence of an exact Singapore-specific or Filipino-specific nutrient record.
notes: 1. The input record labels this food as 'Filipino' cuisine; however, fried wonton balls are of Cantonese Chinese origin and are widely served across Singapore and the Philippines. The cuisine field has been updated to reflect this dual heritage. 2. The food_group has been reclassified from 'Grains and staples' to 'Snacks' as fried wonton balls are consumed as a snack or appetiser rather than a staple grain. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy fried wonton entry. 4. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.1 g per 100 g based on trace amounts from frying oil; this is a low but non-zero value consistent with deep-fried wheat products. 5. GI is an analogous estimate (GI 55, low) based on University of Sydney GI Database data for fried wheat-wrapped dumplings; no direct measurement for fried wonton balls was found. 6. Halal status is marked false as the filling typically contains pork; individual restaurant preparations may vary. 7. The input description 'Golden bites with savory flavor explosions!' is promotional and has been replaced with a factual nutritional and culinary description.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft built. Identified that food_group 'Grains and staples' was inappropriate; reclassified to 'Snacks'. Promotional description replaced with factual nutritional description.

Iteration 2: Validator feedback indicated q4_glycemic_completeness failure: gi_category 'medium' did not match glycemic_index 55 (expected low). Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' as GI 55 falls into the low GI category. Updated health_context for Type 2 Diabetes, llm_training description, and relevant notes to reflect the low GI classification. Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases list. Confirmed vitamin_d_mcg -1 documented in notes. Confirmed GI analogous estimate basis fully documented. Confirmed all multilingual names present for Singaporean cuisine category. Final self-score: 8/8, all hard gates pass. Decision: PASS.
